presentation_variants.presenting_complaint: I've become increasingly short of breath over the past few weeks
Breathing feels harder than usual and I have chest discomfort
I get winded walking short distances now
I feel pressure on one side of my chest and I'm breathless
My breathing has been getting worse gradually

history_data.hpi: Patient presents with a 2-week history of progressively worsening dyspnoea on exertion, now limiting activity significantly. Reports a dull aching discomfort in the right lower chest, worse with deep inspiration. No acute pleuritic chest pain. Exercise tolerance has declined from walking 500 metres to barely managing 50 metres on flat ground. Associated dry cough and mild fatigue. No fever or purulent sputum. Patient has a background of treated breast cancer (completed chemotherapy 18 months ago, thought to be in remission) and is a former smoker. Unintentional weight loss of approximately 5kg over the past 2 months.
patient_quotes: Walking even short distances leaves me breathless now.
There's a dull heaviness on the right side of my chest.
I can feel pressure when I breathe deeply.
My breathing has been getting worse for about two weeks.
I've been losing weight without trying — I thought it was stress.
pmh_pool: Breast cancer — right-sided, treated with lumpectomy and chemotherapy 18 months ago, thought to be in remission
Former smoker — quit 10 years ago, 20 pack-year history
medications_pool: Tamoxifen 20mg daily (adjuvant hormone therapy post-breast cancer)

social_history.smoking: Ex-smoker — quit 10 years ago, 20 pack-year history
social_history.exercise: Previously active — now severely limited by breathlessness
social_history.stress: Significant — anxious about possible cancer recurrence

core_facts.chest_discomfort: dull aching right lower chest — worse with deep inspiration

negative_history: No leg swelling or calf pain
No symptoms of heart failure — no orthopnoea, PND, or ankle swelling
No TB contacts or recent travel to endemic areas

respiratory: Stony dullness to percussion — right lung base extending to mid zone
Markedly reduced breath sounds — right lower and mid zone
Bronchial breathing at upper border of effusion — right mid zone
Left lung clear — normal breath sounds throughout

investigations.Chest X-Ray: Large right-sided pleural effusion with blunting of the right costophrenic angle and opacification of the right lower and mid zones. Tracheal and mediastinal shift to the left — consistent with a large effusion. No left-sided effusion. No pneumothorax. Cardiac silhouette normal size. Appearances raise strong suspicion for malignant pleural effusion in the context of known breast cancer history.
investigations.CT Chest: Large right pleural effusion confirmed. Multiple right-sided pleural nodules and pleural thickening — highly suspicious for malignant pleural seeding. 2.1cm right lower lobe pulmonary nodule. Right hilar lymphadenopathy. No pericardial effusion. No left pleural effusion. Findings are consistent with metastatic disease — likely recurrence of known breast cancer.
investigations.Ultrasound Abdomen: Performed as part of staging assessment. No hepatic metastases identified. No ascites. No abdominal lymphadenopathy. Normal-sized kidneys bilaterally.
investigations.FBC (Full Blood Count): WBC 9.8 × 10⁹/L, Hb 10.2 g/dL (normocytic anaemia — anaemia of chronic disease in context of malignancy), MCV 83 fL, Platelets 398 × 10⁹/L (reactive thrombocytosis). Anaemia requires iron studies.
investigations.CRP: 52 mg/L — elevated, consistent with malignancy-associated inflammation.
investigations.LFTs (Liver Function Tests): ALP 212 IU/L (elevated — may indicate bony or hepatic metastases), ALT 36 IU/L, Bilirubin 12 µmol/L, Albumin 26 g/L (hypoalbuminaemia — malnutrition and chronic disease). Elevated ALP warrants bone scan or further staging.
investigations.Urea & Electrolytes: Na 133 mmol/L (mild hyponatraemia — monitor), K 3.9 mmol/L, Creatinine 78 µmol/L, Urea 5.8 mmol/L — normal renal function.
investigations.BNP: 58 pg/mL — within normal limits. Cardiac failure is an unlikely cause of this effusion. Supports exudative rather than transudative aetiology.
investigations.Coagulation Screen (PT/APPT/INR): PT 13s, APTT 28s, INR 1.1 — normal. Important baseline prior to thoracentesis.

hidden_diagnosis: Malignant Pleural Effusion — Likely Metastatic Breast Cancer Recurrence

patient_rules.emotional_state: anxious and fearful — aware that symptoms may represent cancer recurrence, trying to stay calm

essential_questions: character and location of chest discomfort — pleuritic vs dull
unintentional weight loss — amount and timeframe
fever or night sweats — suggest infection or lymphoma
history of malignancy — type, treatment, remission status
history of cardiac disease — orthopnoea, PND, ankle swelling to consider heart failure
history of chronic kidney disease — consider transudative causes
leg swelling or calf pain — exclude PE
TB exposure or travel to endemic areas
essential_examinations: general assessment — cachexia, pallor, breathlessness at rest vs exertion
respiratory examination — percussion (stony dull), auscultation (absent breath sounds), chest expansion asymmetry, bronchial breathing at effusion border
cardiovascular — JVP, peripheral oedema (heart failure vs malignant effusion)
lymph node examination — supraclavicular, cervical, axillary nodes
critical_actions: recognise pleural effusion from clinical signs — stony dullness and absent breath sounds
obtain chest X-ray to confirm and characterise effusion size
arrange CT chest to evaluate for pleural nodules, malignancy, and underlying lung pathology
distinguish transudative from exudative effusion — Light's criteria applied to pleural fluid
arrange diagnostic thoracentesis — send pleural fluid for: protein, LDH, glucose, pH, cytology, MC&S, AFB if TB suspected
in context of known breast cancer — assume malignant until proven otherwise
check BNP and echocardiogram if cardiac failure is a differential
urgent oncology referral given previous breast cancer history and suspicious CT findings
therapeutic thoracentesis if large effusion causing significant dyspnoea
consider indwelling pleural catheter (IPC) or pleurodesis for recurrent malignant effusion
staging investigations — CT abdomen/pelvis, bone scan or PET-CT
check coagulation before any pleural procedure
red_flags: History of malignancy with new pleural effusion — malignant effusion until proven otherwise
Unintentional weight loss — systemic feature of malignancy or TB
Stony dullness to percussion — distinguishes effusion from consolidation
Mediastinal shift away from effusion — large effusion causing haemodynamic concern
Pleural nodules on CT — strongly suggest malignant aetiology
Hypoalbuminaemia — may cause transudative effusion but also suggests malignant cachexia
Elevated ALP — bony or hepatic metastases
acceptable_differentials: Transudative pleural effusion from heart failure — but no cardiac history, normal BNP, no peripheral oedema
Parapneumonic effusion — no fever, no consolidation, no purulent sputum
Tuberculosis — chronic course, night sweats, TB exposure; pleural fluid AFB and adenosine deaminase would help
Mesothelioma — asbestos exposure, pleural thickening; biopsy required to differentiate
Pulmonary embolism with haemorrhagic effusion — consider if sudden onset, pleuritic pain, PE risk factors
Lymphoma — mediastinal involvement, B symptoms; biopsy required
management_actions: Chest X-Ray — confirm effusion and assess size
CT Chest with contrast — characterise effusion, detect pleural nodules, assess lungs and mediastinum
Ultrasound-guided diagnostic thoracentesis — aspirate pleural fluid for analysis
Pleural fluid: protein, LDH, glucose, pH — apply Light's criteria to classify as exudate or transudate
Pleural fluid cytology — identify malignant cells
Pleural fluid MC&S and AFB — exclude infection and TB
BNP — if cardiac failure suspected as aetiology
FBC, LFTs, U&E, CRP — baseline labs and staging screen
Coagulation screen before any pleural procedure
Therapeutic thoracentesis if large symptomatic effusion — drain up to 1.5L per session
Oncology referral — given breast cancer history and CT findings
CT abdomen/pelvis and bone scan or PET-CT — full staging
Consider indwelling pleural catheter (IPC) for recurrent symptomatic malignant effusion
MDT discussion before further oncological treatment decisions
Nutritional support — dietitian referral given weight loss and hypoalbuminaemia
critical_history_questions: When did you first notice the breathlessness — how has it changed?
What can you do now compared to 2–3 weeks ago?
Do you have any chest discomfort — where exactly, and does it change with breathing?
Have you had any cough — dry or productive?
Have you coughed up any blood?
Have you lost any weight recently without trying — how much and over how long?
Have you had a fever, chills, or night sweats?
You mentioned a previous cancer — can you tell me more about your breast cancer treatment and when you were last told you were in remission?
Have you had any heart problems — swollen ankles, waking at night breathless, needing extra pillows to sleep?
Any leg swelling or calf pain?
Have you ever been exposed to asbestos?
Have you been in contact with anyone with tuberculosis or travelled to a TB-endemic area?
Do you smoke or have you smoked in the past?
